title: Maestro Module Showcase
summary: A comprehensive example exercising every module type the Maestro planning server supports. Use this file as a visual regression test and reference for plan authors.

items.text: Every module type renders without visual defects
All interactive elements (checkboxes, links, message threading) function correctly
Mobile layout does not break for any module type
Empty items lists degrade gracefully with a placeholder message
Review the module type catalog and schema definitions
Create JSON plan fixture covering all nine types
Verify each template renders against this fixture
Test grilling wizard prompt display and option interaction
Run cross-browser visual snapshots and document any gaps
A new module type is added to the schema but forgotten in the template dispatcher
items.impact: New module falls through to the generic renderer, missing its custom UI
items.mitigation: Keep this fixture exhaustive — add a module for every registered type so the gap is caught visually

items.text: Column key changes in the model break the table template
items.impact: Table cells render empty because the Go field name no longer matches the key switch
items.mitigation: This fixture includes a table module with all known column keys; any mismatch surfaces during review

items.text: TOON encoding round-trips drop optional fields
items.impact: Fields like hideRowNum or rationale survive JSON but not the TOON→Plan path
items.mitigation: Validate via automated round-trip test that compares the decoded Plan against the original JSON

items.text: The Maestro server is running against the standard plans directory
All templates under templates/components/ are up to date with model.go
The browser supports WebSocket for live reload during visual review
maestro/plans/module-showcase.json
maestro/plans/grilling-wizard-demo.json
maestro/templates/components/module-table.html
maestro/templates/components/plan-module.html
maestro/model.go
docs/maestro/module-reference.md
Include a table module in the showcase fixture
items.rationale: The table type is the only module that uses the Module-level Columns field and HideRowNum flag. Excluding it would leave a gap in template coverage.

items.text: Store showcase and grilling-demo as separate plan files
items.options: single combined file; two separate files
items.rationale: The grilling wizard demo needs a message thread with prompt payloads, which is a fundamentally different document shape. Two files keep each focused and make it obvious which features each one exercises.

items.text: The table module is the only type that reads from Module.Columns and Module.HideRowNum — all other types work purely with Item fields.
The changes module uses JSON key "type" which maps to Go field ChangeType. The FlatItem/FlatPlan serialization uses "changeType" to avoid colliding with the module-level "type" key.
If a new module type is added to model.go, it must also get a case in plan-module.html's dispatcher or it will fall through to module-generic.
Should the table module support inline editing of cell values?
items.answer: No — the plan page is read-only. Editing happens via the API.

items.text: Should we add a timeline / gantt module type for scheduling?
Should the generic fallback renderer emit a console warning about the unknown type?
items.answer: Yes — a visible badge is better than silently rendering items without type-specific UI.
